verdict_reasoning: STIM1 is a type-1 transmembrane protein anchored in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane. Its canonical role is as an ER Ca2+ sensor that activates store-operated Ca2+ entry (SOCE) by gating plasma-membrane Orai channels — a process occurring at ER-PM junctional compartments. While the bulk of STIM1 resides in the ER, it undergoes documented Ca2+-dependent translocation and clustering at ER-PM contact sites during SOCE activation, bringing it into close physical apposition with the PM and enabling direct interaction with Orai1 at the outer leaflet during the activated state. Additionally, STIM1 has documented cell-state-induced and cancer-associated surface display: cancer cells exhibit ectopic STIM1 on the outer leaflet (ecto-STIM1), and activation-induced rapid translocation to PM-proximal compartments occurs during immune-cell stimulation. The protein's primary anchor is the ER, but its dual-localization cycling to ER-PM junctions during activation, combined with documented ecto-pool in cancer contexts, places it in the contextual category rather than strictly intracellular.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Ambiguity over whether ER-PM junctional clustering and transient juxtamembrane apposition during SOCE activation constitutes sufficient 'outer-leaflet' accessibility for binder engagement. Direct PM integration does not occur; the protein remains ER-anchored but moves to within nanometers of the PM during activation. Cancer-cell ecto-STIM1 is documented but may be minor relative to canonical ER pool.
